schools.summary: The practical application of chapter 1 ('therefore,' because the Son is so great): 'we ought to give the more earnest heed (prosechein — attend more closely, pay greater attention) to the things which we have heard (the gospel)' — the surpassing dignity of the Son making the more imperative a close attention to his message; 'lest at any time we should let them slip (pararyomen — drift away, flow past, slip away, as a ship that drifts past the harbour, or water from a leaky vessel)' — the danger not chiefly violent apostasy but a gradual, careless drifting from the truth (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Therefore we ought to give the more earnest heed to the things which we have heard
lest at any time we should let them slip
the first warning: heed the gospel lest we drift
the peril of drifting away

schools.summary: The premise of the warning (an argument from the less to the greater): 'for if the word spoken by angels (the law, given through the mediation of angels, cf. Acts 7:53; Galatians 3:19) was stedfast (bebaios — firm, binding, valid)' — the law, mediated by angels, firm and authoritative; 'and every transgression (overstepping) and disobedience (refusal to hear) received a just recompence (endikon misthapodosian — a deserved penalty)' — the law punishing every violation justly; the a fortiori force being that if even the law (given through angels, inferior to the Son) was so binding, how much more the gospel spoken by the Son himself (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: if the word spoken by angels was stedfast
every transgression and disobedience received a just recompence of reward
the law spoken by angels punished every transgression
the argument from less to greater

schools.summary: The climax of the warning: 'how shall we escape (the just penalty), if we neglect (amelesantes — disregard, make light of, are careless of) so great salvation?' — the rhetorical question with no answer, there being no escape for those who neglect the gospel, 'neglect' (not violent rejection but mere careless disregard) being itself fatal; the salvation 'so great' (surpassing the law in its author, blessings, and cost, the greater the salvation the graver the neglect); 'which at the first began to be spoken by the Lord (Jesus himself), and was confirmed (made sure, guaranteed) unto us by them that heard him (the apostles, eyewitnesses)' — first proclaimed by the Lord, then surely transmitted (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: How shall we escape, if we neglect so great salvation
which at the first began to be spoken by the Lord, and was confirmed unto us by them that heard him
the peril of neglecting so great a salvation
neglect itself leaves no escape

schools.summary: God added his own testimony to that of the eyewitnesses (v.3): 'God also bearing them witness (synepimartyrountos — joining his testimony, attesting alongside), both with signs (semeiois — significant tokens) and wonders (terasin — marvels), and with divers miracles (poikilais dynamesin — various mighty works), and gifts (merismois — distributions, apportionings) of the Holy Ghost, according to his own (God's) will' — God confirming the gospel by a threefold display of supernatural power and by the charismatic gifts of the Spirit distributed as he willed, authenticating the apostolic witness and leaving the hearers without excuse (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: God also bearing them witness, both with signs and wonders, and with divers miracles
and gifts of the Holy Ghost, according to his own will
God's confirming witness to the gospel
attested by signs and the Spirit's gifts

schools.summary: The return to the theme of the Son's superiority over angels: 'for unto the angels hath he not put in subjection (hypetaxen — subjected, made subordinate) the world to come (ten oikoumenen ten mellousan — the future order, the Messianic age and its kingdom), whereof we speak' — the coming world (the new order inaugurated by Christ, the gospel dispensation and the kingdom of the Son, the very subject of the epistle) not given to the rule of angels but subjected to the Son and, in him, to redeemed man (vv.6-9) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: For unto the angels hath he not put in subjection the world to come, whereof we speak
the world to come subjected not to angels but to the Son
the Messianic age and kingdom
not given to angels' rule

schools.summary: The quotation of Psalm 8:4-6: 'but one in a certain place testified' (the writer's manner of citation, naming neither author nor place, the source being well known), cited to prove that dominion over the coming world is given to man, not angels; the psalmist's wonder, 'what is man (enosh — frail man), that thou art mindful (rememberest, carest for) of him? or the son of man (the descendant of Adam), that thou visitest (lookest upon, regardest with favour) him?' — astonishment that the great God should so regard frail, lowly man; the Psalm speaking first of man in general (the dignity God gave Adam) but applied pre-eminently to the Son of Man, Christ (v.9), in whom man's destiny is fulfilled (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: What is man, that thou art mindful of him? or the son of man, that thou visitest him?
the psalmist's wonder that God is mindful of man
Psalm 8 applied to man and the Son of Man
the wonder of God's regard for frail man

schools.summary: The description from the Psalm: 'thou madest him a little (brachy ti — a little, in degree, or 'for a little while') lower than the angels' — man made in rank somewhat below the angels (the phrase later applied to Christ's temporary humiliation, v.9); 'thou crownedst him with glory and honour' — God crowning man with dignity and majesty, the high rank given at creation; 'and didst set him over the works of thy hands' — man given rule over the creation (Genesis 1:28), the dominion that, lost in Adam, is restored and fulfilled in Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Thou madest him a little lower than the angels; thou crownedst him with glory and honour
and didst set him over the works of thy hands
man made a little lower than the angels yet crowned with glory
the dignity and dominion of man

schools.summary: The climax of the Psalm's grant: 'thou hast put all things in subjection under his feet' — the dominion given to man universal, all things placed under him (the figure of complete subjugation); 'for in that he put all in subjection under him, he left nothing that is not put under him' — the grant excluding nothing, the dominion total and without exception in principle; 'but now we see not yet all things put under him' — in fact man does not now exercise this universal dominion, it is not yet realized, the gap between the promise and the present being filled by Jesus (v.9), in whom the destiny is begun and guaranteed (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Thou hast put all things in subjection under his feet... he left nothing that is not put under him
But now we see not yet all things put under him
all things subjected to man in principle, not yet seen in fact
the unrealized dominion fulfilled in Jesus

schools.summary: What we do not see in man we see in Jesus: he 'was made a little (brachy ti — a little, or for a little while) lower than the angels for (because of, with a view to) the suffering of death' — his temporary humiliation below the angels for the sake of dying — and is now 'crowned with glory and honour,' exalted as a reward for his sufferings (Philippians 2:9), the Psalm fulfilled in him. On the text: the received reading 'by the grace of God (chariti theou)' — Christ tasted death by God's gracious purpose and favour — is almost certainly correct, against the variant 'apart from God (choris theou),' anciently widespread (known to Origen and many fathers, favoured later by Bengel, in two late MSS.) but rejected by Ellicott and Robertson. On the extent: Barnes presses that 'taste death for every man (hyper pantos — for each and all)' affirms universal atonement in the plainest terms — Christ died for all without exception, and the doctrine of limited atonement must modify the plain Scripture language.

Clarke: Christ took the poisoned cup out of every man's hand and drank it for each — 'every man, all human creatures'; the atonement is for the whole human race. Wesley: 'for every man that ever was or will be born into the world.' Both read the universal extent of the atonement directly from the text.

Calvin restricts 'every man (hyper pantos)' to 'man' as mentioned in v.6 — that is, all the faithful, to whom the dominion lost in Adam is restored, not as fallen but as made righteous by faith. Robertson notes the mediating formula of Andrew Fuller: the death of Christ was 'sufficient for all, efficient for some'; the early Greek theologians wrongly took 'every' as 'everything.'

debates.question: Did Christ taste death 'for every man' — universal or particular atonement?

sides.position: Universal — Christ died for all men without exception (the plain sense of 'every man')
Particular — 'every man' means all the faithful/believers; sufficient for all, efficient for some

debates.question: Is the reading 'by the grace of God' (chariti) or 'apart from God' (choris)?

sides.position: 'By the grace of God' (chariti theou) — the received and almost certainly correct reading
'Apart from God' (choris theou) — anciently widespread (Origen, many fathers, Bengel), but rejected
holders: a minority reading, noted but not accepted

themes: we see Jesus, who was made a little lower than the angels for the suffering of death, crowned with glory and honour
that he by the grace of God should taste death for every man
Jesus crowned through death, tasting death for every man
the atonement: by grace, and for whom

schools.summary: The plan of salvation through a suffering Saviour was fitting to God's character: 'it became (was fitting, suitable to) him, for whom (the final cause) are all things, and by whom (the efficient cause) are all things' — God, the end and author of all, acting fittingly; 'in bringing many sons unto glory' — God's purpose to lead many sons (the redeemed, made his children) to glory; 'to make the captain (archegon — author, leader, pioneer, the one who goes first) of their salvation perfect (teleiosai — complete, fit, consummate) through sufferings' — Christ made perfect not morally (he was sinless) but as Saviour, fully fitted and consummated as the author of salvation through what he suffered (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Christ, the captain and pioneer of salvation, was 'perfected' through sufferings — consummated and qualified for his saving office, going first through suffering and death that he might lead the many sons to glory; his perfecting is the completion of his work as Saviour, not a making-good of any defect.

themes: it became him, for whom are all things, and by whom are all things, in bringing many sons unto glory
to make the captain of their salvation perfect through sufferings
God perfecting the captain of salvation through sufferings
Christ fitted as Saviour by suffering

schools.summary: The ground of the brotherhood: 'for both he that sanctifieth (Christ, who makes holy) and they who are sanctified (the believers, made holy) are all of one (ex henos — of one source, one nature, one Father, or one stock)' — Christ and his people sharing a common origin and nature, he truly one with them; whether 'of one' means of one Father (God) or one common humanity, the point being the real union and kinship between the Saviour and the saved; 'for which cause he is not ashamed to call them brethren' (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Christ, though so exalted, does not disdain to own his people as brothers; the glorious Son acknowledges the kinship with those he sanctifies, an act of wonderful condescension and love, having made himself one with them in nature.

themes: both he that sanctifieth and they who are sanctified are all of one
he is not ashamed to call them brethren
Christ and his people all of one
the exalted Son owning his people as brethren

schools.summary: The citation (Psalm 22:22, the great Messianic psalm of the cross): 'I will declare thy name unto my brethren, in the midst of the church will I sing praise unto thee' — Christ, in the words of the Psalm, calling his people 'brethren' and promising to make known God's name to them (the office of the incarnate Son to reveal the Father), and singing God's praise in the midst of the congregation — owning the kinship and one with his people in worship (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Christ, in the midst of the church, leads the praise of his people to God; he is one with them in worship, the chief singer in the assembly of the brethren, making the Father known to those he is not ashamed to call his own.

themes: I will declare thy name unto my brethren
in the midst of the church will I sing praise unto thee
Christ declaring God's name among his brethren
one with his people in worship

schools.summary: The second and third citations (Isaiah 8:17-18): 'I will put my trust in him' — Christ, as man, exercising trust in God, sharing the believer's posture of faith, one with his brethren in depending on God (his trusting showing his true humanity and union with his people); 'Behold I and the children which God hath given me' — Christ presenting himself with the 'children' God gave him (the redeemed) as a family, he and his people one household, the children entrusted to him by the Father (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Christ stands with the children God has given him as one family; sharing their faith ('I will put my trust in him') and owning them as his household, he proves himself truly one with the men he came to save.

themes: I will put my trust in him
Behold I and the children which God hath given me
Christ's trust and his children
one with his people in faith and family

schools.summary: The reason for the incarnation: 'forasmuch then as the children (those he came to save) are partakers of (share in) flesh and blood (human nature), he also himself likewise took part of (shared, partook of) the same' — because his people are human, the Son himself took true human nature, the incarnation grounded in his union with the children; 'that through death (his own dying) he might destroy (katargese — bring to nought, render powerless, annul) him that had the power (the dominion, the sway) of death, that is, the devil' — by dying, Christ broke the power of the one who wielded death, the means of victory being death itself (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the one destroyed is the devil — Satan, who through sin and death held men in his grip; Christ's death broke Satan's power over death, defeating the enemy by entering and conquering his own domain, that the children might be freed.

themes: as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, he also himself likewise took part of the same
that through death he might destroy him that had the power of death, that is, the devil
Christ sharing flesh and blood to destroy the devil
conquering death by death

schools.summary: The further purpose of Christ's victory: 'and deliver (set free, release) them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage (douleias — slavery)' — Christ freeing those whom the dread of death had held in lifelong slavery; the 'fear of death' (the terror of dying, with its dread of judgment and the unknown) holding men in bondage all their lives, a tyranny from which they cannot free themselves; by destroying the power of death and the devil (v.14), Christ delivers his people from this fear, deliverance from the fear of death being a fruit of the atonement (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: deliver them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage
Christ delivering those enslaved by the fear of death
freed from the terror of dying
a fruit of the atonement

schools.summary: Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson, with Chrysostom and Clarke, prefer the marginal reading: 'he taketh not hold of (epilambanetai) angels, but he taketh hold of the seed of Abraham' — the verb meaning to lay hold of in order to help, rescue, or succour (as in Mark 8:23; Acts 23:19; cf. boethesai, v.18), Christ laying hold of fallen man to save him, not the fallen angels. Chrysostom: as human nature fled, Christ pursued and laid hold of it (derived from the figure of pursuing those who flee). Clarke: Christ seized the falling human creature and prevented his endless fall, but did not lay hold of the falling angels. 'The seed of Abraham' being the literal descendants (fitting for Jewish readers) or, more broadly, the spiritual seed, the children of faith (Galatians 3:29; Robertson).

Gill and Calvin follow the received rendering 'he took not on him the nature of angels; but... the seed of Abraham' — Christ assumed human nature, not the nature of angels (there being no redemption for fallen angels, and an angelic nature being incapable of the death needed to atone). Calvin presses this against Marcion, Manicheus, and the docetists who denied Christ a real human body: the Apostle speaks of nature, that Christ, clothed with flesh, was real man begotten of human seed, one person in two natures (against Nestorius too).

debates.question: Does the verse mean Christ 'took not the nature of angels' or 'taketh not hold of angels (to help)'?

sides.position: 'He took not on him the nature of angels but of man' — Christ assumed human, not angelic, nature
'He taketh hold (to help/rescue) not of angels but of the seed of Abraham' — the verb means to lay hold of to save

themes: he took not on him the nature of angels; but he took on him the seed of Abraham
Christ took human nature, not angelic
the rendering crux: took nature, or took hold to help
God's love to man over angels

schools.summary: The conclusion: 'wherefore in all things (in every respect, except sin) it behoved (was necessary, fitting) him to be made like unto his brethren' — Christ had to be fully human, like those he came to save, the incarnation a necessity for his saving office; 'that he might be a merciful (compassionate, sympathizing) and faithful (trustworthy, reliable) high priest in things pertaining to God' — his full humanity fitting him to be the high priest, merciful toward men and faithful toward God (the first mention of the priesthood that is the epistle's grand subject); 'to make reconciliation for (hilaskesthai — to make propitiation, atonement for) the sins of the people' — the high priest's office to atone for sin (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: in all things it behoved him to be made like unto his brethren
that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make reconciliation for the sins of the people
Christ made like his brethren to be high priest
the atoning high priest

schools.summary: The ground of Christ's sympathy: 'for in that he himself hath suffered being tempted (peirastheis — having been tried, tested, and suffered in his testing)' — Christ, in his humanity, really suffered under temptation and trial (in the wilderness, in Gethsemane, throughout his life), knowing by experience what it is to be tempted, his sympathy not theoretical but founded on his own suffering; 'he is able to succour (boethesai — help, run to the aid of, bring timely relief to) them that are tempted' — because he has felt temptation, he can sympathize with and help his people in theirs, the tempted Saviour the able helper of the tempted (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: in that he himself hath suffered being tempted, he is able to succour them that are tempted
the tempted Christ able to succour the tempted
sympathy founded on his own suffering
the able helper of the tested
